item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was like, this guy sounds like trump. And it only got better

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want you to know that when that I click the "collapsible" and lifelock opened in another tab, I literally said "God, stop!" out loud. I love when SCP makes me afraid of things that I've never feared before. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I've heard of Target but how do I actually locate one of the stores?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having had the benefit of reading the prior comments, I think this is in a good place in terms of being "topical." I initially got Donald Trump vibes off the interview log but did not realize it was explicitly intended until reading the comments and counting the black-barred characters.

Making the connection more explicit might anchor it too heavily to that cultural knowledge and risk having this age poorly. As it stands, I think you have a fairly timeless skip. It will still be good even when we no longer have the cultural understanding of Trump that we do now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Terrifying! Thanks

no but really it's an effective tapping of what I think is nowadays a pretty common fear. Just this morning Twitter rolled out some update that broke the method I was using to keep "sponsored tweets" off my timeline. The Security tab that actually leads to that identity protection site is a nice touch.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I saw that Target logo in the middle of the first tab, I knew I had to upvote. Pretty well-executed piece with some interesting considerations. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorites in series 5 probably the best bit was the fact that the advertisements slowly seeped into the article itself. I laughed out loud at the :Input Level 5 security credentials because i totally did not see it coming. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should have rick rolled when you had the chance

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Soundtrack for this SCP: "Fashion Stigmata", by Spray.

Also, since when have SVGs been capable of animation?! Nice subtle touch. :-o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty creepy, but Miguel is really annoying, and their decrying of solar power reminded me more of a climate-change-denying radical conservative politician than a warning from the future or whatever. Enjoyed the formatting, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god, "Thaumiel" level ad blocker?

+1 heartily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that you mention it, I think it might have been more effective to put the media for the specific ads in the same tab as the descriptions. I don't know if that's feasible or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the justification of your vote. As an aside, the only reason the movie star is a child is because the x-ray image in the gallery is one of a kid (you can tell for sure by the excess teeth in the mandible) and so I wanted to be accurate regarding that. No edginess intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted this before I got to the phone calls, and it never quite left there.

+ show block
– hide block

The idea of anomalous ads appearing in strange places isn't exactly original (I'm pretty sure it's the basis for multiple other SCPs), and just making them show up everywhere, omnipotently and indiscriminately, kind of sapped it of any identity. Ditto them being for normal/current brands.

Focusing so hard on how difficult they were to remove 1) made the description excessively long with uninteresting detail and 2) just felt like a desperate attempt to make this more interesting by making it more dangerous. The super-edgey nature of some of the ads (We hurt a child star! We're gonna blow up the Taj Mahal!) enforced this feeling. (And, like, the "dangerous =/= interesting" truism is still true here: none of that made me care more.) Plus that list of ads really wasn't very interesting. The placement of ads in the article was occasionally pretty amusing, but nothing new and ultimately didn't do much for this article.

The fucking gonzo conversation between the Foundation and the PoI and the vision of the future presented was… I mean, it gave me mixed feelings. It was definitely the high point. I think it struck a good balance on the difficult PoI-who-is-Foundation-savvy tightrope, and the vision of the future was pretty interesting. The literal Trump-isms were, to say the least, not a writing choice that worked for me.

I guess this kind of ties back to my critique of the anomaly: if the core identity of the anomaly is "big, violent, and stupid", that definitely fits the PoI, but that's also a boring identity for an SCP. That left the whole article (including some excess text) relying on the character/interactions part to carry the weight, and, for me, they didn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok thank you for the suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wikimedia Commons permits hotlinking images, but explicitly recommends against it: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Commons:Reusing_content_outside_Wikimedia/technical#Hotlinking

You should probably change the hotlinked ads. If nothing else, it'd reduce temptation for someone to go vandalize them to indirectly alter this.

item.title: Bahahahah!
item.content:encoded: This was a great use of medium! A reminder to all that there is always new ways to change it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’ve added a section in the Gallery that displays pictures recovered from the discarded burner phones, presumed to be ads developed/deployed by PoI-4000-1.

I hope this doesn’t change anyone’s opinion drastically, but there it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well good to know it is still there. I don't think I'll edit it anymore.

"Trumpectomy"…nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who read it post-Trumpism-ectomy, I still got it by the end. The name "Miguel" threw me for a bit, but the little subtle nods made it inescapable.

item.title: Re: Measurements and Units
item.content:encoded: This is a great catch and some good, liberating advice. The units and measurements have been simplified and corrected.

item.title: Measurements and Units
item.content:encoded:
Great Concept, and the dialogue is exactly as choppy and incoherent as it should be, if I'm reading it correctly. But a huge flaw with this one is the given measurements for everything. I am all for making up numbers to give the appearance of scientific rigor. However, the particular made up numbers, as they are now, are inaccurate enough to affect my suspension of disbelief.

Examples:

  1. Instance 14 - The size given is 0.5 m by 0.2 m. The description says that it was on a child's forehead. My forehead measures 18 cm by 9 cm, and mine is made bigger by my receding hairline.
  2. Instance 30 - This one might be a case of "Ignore the ignorant guy," but I've never heard of s as a unit of arclength, and the 28 might need units. It might make sense to say that it was so many steradians, which are the unitless units of solid angle, and then in the description give the height off the ground. The best option though is probably just to say it was so many meters by so many meters. Keep it simple.
  3. Instance 51 - I don't know how large Agent Belopolsky is, but if his lung nodes have diameters ranging from .4 m to .6 m, he's at least 80 cm wide. I'm fairly skinny, but at my widest I'm significantly less than 50 cm. And as you get fatter, your lungs don't grow much.
  4. All over - Keeping units simple. Instead of 0.5 m, say 50 cm. Also, a radius isn't measured in m2. That's just m.
  5. All over - Make your approximate approximate. This is a very easy mistake to make. There's a desire to be very precise when writing science fiction, and that's OK. But for this type of summary document, precision isn't necessary. And precision is downright wrong when preceded by "approximately" or by "~" (which is generally math short-hand for approximately). So, In every case where getting an exact measurement doesn't make sense (pixel size of digital media does, size of a floating hologram does not), get rid of decimals. When you measure something that's over 100 m in length, you aren't going to measure to centimeter precision.

Overall, I think there's some strong potential here, but you need to be more careful with your selection of numbers and units before you get a +1 from me. As they are they drag me right out of the fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok will do this tonight.

Edit:
+ show block
– hide block

Main image:
Hot Air Balloon + SCP Foundation insignia

Logos:
General Electric Oil & Gas
Target
Yum!
Smoking Cessation Ad
Listerine Logo
Atlas Jam Company
Duckworth & Co. Hop Ale
Boost Mobile

Gallery:
Penn State Football + Billboard
USA Travel Ad + Picture of sky taken by me
Taj Mahal + Nissan
Kobe + McDonald’s
X-Ray of head + Coke
X-ray of lungs + Star of Life
Matterhorn + Evian

Burner phone pictures:
5 Hour + Pulling the strings
Miss Universe + Death mask + Clean & Clear
Early Epilepsy Treatment + Eternity + Beauty is forever + Religion + Comcast
Axe + Lips
Salvation + Hydrocodone + NEEDle & Syringe

Misc:
iPhone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit was Miguel frustrating, which is the point, and I applaud you for creating such an emotional reaction via some rather excellent characterization. The tie into the history of advertising was also clever, as was the slow descent into ads popping up within the Foundation database.

While there are entries that make a better use of the History Theme, this article is pretty noteworthy and does a good job doing its own thing.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All images are from Wiki Commons.

Good, but it'd be useful if you could identify them a bit more specifically, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh nice.
– hide block

Gonna give this a read. Little factoid: I used "Miguel" because in this timeline, one of Trump's descendants marries and has children with a Hispanic immigrant whose family was at one point subject to his discrimination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The urge to reach through the screen and strangle Miguel rose exponentially the more I read. I'm torn between thinking that he is, at least partially, lying about the future in order to get the Foundation to buy ad space (since what he's saying seems so clearly angled to annoy the Foundation and give them a reason to buy space), and suspecting that he may actually be too stupid to concoct such a lie convincingly. Very nice work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Miguel, you say?

Seriously though, well done. I enjoyed it from start to finish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is the kind of meta i love to see, love ur skips so far!! 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love everything about this. It starts with a simple, creepy anomaly, but through the examples and then especially the interviews, you masterfully peel back the layers of a future adpocalypse dystopia. A scare I haven't experienced through this site yet. The humor and meta advertisements throughout are frosting on the cake. Awesome stuff, kudos!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yeah this is fucked up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I ended up taking out all the really overt Tumpisms. Thank you for the input.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I'm not sure, and I'm loathe to push you either way — a lot of this stuff boils down to a confusing sort of alchemy for which there are never any clear 'right' answers. All I can tell you is how I would have done this — but I'm not at all sure how I would have done it is any better than how you already did it, so take the following with prescription-grade salt:

+ show block
– hide block

I think the meandering style of the dialogue is great, in that it works to convince us that this guy is 1) An idiot, 2) Very powerful, important, and unchallenged in his time, and 3) The natural consequence of a culture where corporatism is our culture.

(Aside: The bit where the O5 starts talking about historical figures in marketing — and Miguel instantly recognizes them, because those are the people who are considered 'historical figures' in his world — was an especially nice touch. In some ways, it felt a little forced, because I think it had already been well-established that this person was a product of a corporate culture — but it was still such a clever way to determine/demonstrate that. Clever enough that I'm loathe to say you should touch it or cut it.)

If I were writing this, I would keep that style, but remove all overt Trumpisms. I'd keep him talking like Trump, but I would write it in such a way that the reader isn't going to recognize it as Trump unless the reader is trying to recognize it as Trump (IE, nothing Trump has ever actually said, and no direct "Trump-isms"). I think the dialogue is strong enough to remain engaging without them (though I would possibly reduce/cut some of the fat to make it quicker — but not a lot, because I feel like the fact that he just keeps going is definitely part of his voice. This is clearly a guy who's accustom to being heard, rather than stopping to listen). I would then keep the black box precisely as it is — to me, that would be an Easter Egg for the reader astute enough to bother and count the number of boxes to match them to Donald Trump's name.

My reasoning for this: I don't think you need the reader to directly connect this to Trump. This article is definitely about a form of Trumpism, but (as counter-intuitive as this might sound) I don't think you actually need to invoke Trump to get across Trumpism. Trumpism is something that probably predates Trump by quite a large margin; he's just the most blatant and obvious example of it in our time.

Again, let me stress — writing is basically alchemy, not science. I think this article is great as-is, and I'm not sure if the way I would have done it is actually any better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block


Thank you. Yeah, I am discovering just how hard it can be to play a Trump-derived character in a subtle way. Subtle is the last adjective for him. I wonder if it may be better actually, and you know I respect your opinion very much, to just make it explicit at the end of the transcripts, no black boxes. Because I am less interested in making it a reveal (doesn’t seem to be working anyway) then I am pressing forward the Trump.

What is your opinion of that turn?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: David Foster Wallace died and went to hell and while he was there he wrote this by way of an Ouija board. I have no other explanation for this article. This is absolutely a horror piece, and I'll be damned if it didn't get that effect across; I haven't had this visceral a reaction to an article in ages, and you use pretty smart writing to wring it out of me. Gleefully upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved how out of control this got; the writing quality was pretty superb throughout. Part of me wished it got even more ridiculous with the extended ads; part of me appreciated the restraint.

The idea of a future war driven by corporations leading legions of employees/customers memetically altered for brand loyalty (and even exploiting time travel to accomplish this task better) is fantastic, and similar to ideas I've wanted to explore with ad-bots (which I find inherently terrifying).

I think my only serious complaint is that Miguel had too many clearly identifiable "Trump-isms" going on; that may vary by taste, though. My instinct tells me that you don't need the reader to positively identify who Miguel is to still enjoy the article, so you can make it more of an Easter Egg — but my instincts have been wrong, before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could have been the Foundation, could have been the trademark tyrants, could have been Miguel backing out and covering his tracks, also could have been SCP-4000 itself. I like this as open for interpretation.

"Adpocalypse"is good, maybe I should change the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a idea, but perhaps make a supplement collaboration page listing notable instances

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So did the Foundation win or lose this one? Given that Miguel's genetic material seems to have vanished, it seems like the Foundation either thwarts the adpocalypse in the future, or that the corporate overlords in the future caught up with Miguel and used their Capitalism Powers™ to erase him from exsistance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see, always learning here. However, different images have their own transition time, to coincide with the average reader making their way through the article. I'll see what I can do to consolidate the code.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a difficult balance. I've had a few readers who didn't catch it, so. I'll keep working for the sweet spot. Thank you.

+ show block
– hide block


Toned it down slightly. I guess my hope would be that even if it is figured out easily, it's still entertaining to read the lines in his voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this, there's a good balance of comedy from the absurd premise and horror from how resonant the absurd premise is with the history of advertising IRL. One thing I found kind of underwhelming is

+ show block
– hide block

the twist that Miguel is a descendant of Trump. I figured that out about a quarter of the way through his interviews based on his manner of speaking, and the more blatant hinting later on just made me go "yes, I get it, get on with it." I would try to make that aspect of the story more subtle, because I'm far from the most observant reader on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty clever, and I +1'd. However, I'm 99% sure you can just use the CSS module to get CSS instead of creating an individual HTML document for each image. RIP my loading time.

Edit: the more I think about this SCP, the more unsettling it becomes. And that's great, because I love some god fringe horror. Weak +1 changed to a strong +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes thank you, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how logos just start showing up in the addenda, and by the end, I began to fear them.

The phone call logs go on rather a long time, though the intent at least gets through. By "O-5", do you mean O5-5?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Yes. Great great grandson technically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’ve taken your good advice and replaced the image with the one you edited. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting. No fancy code going on there. Alright I’ll work with this. Hopefully it doesn’t sink the article.

Update: Converted the table to a list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this quite a bit. And I promise that's not at all influenced by the crosslink to Schulman devices…

I had issues with the top image. The perspective is not realistic at all because the logo doesn't bend with the shape of the balloon. Since I have the privilege of Photoshop access and can do such things, I went ahead and made you one with the logo bulged outward and a displacement map to make the sewn edges look more realistic. Feel free to use it or not use it but I'm hoping it's more believable than the current one. I don't think the placement is perfect, but the photograph angle is a little difficult for perceiving how a circle would look on that surface, so I threw in some shading changes for good measure. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This a mix of clever and absolutely fucking bonkers, and I love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So let me guess this straight.

Donald Trump's Great-Great-Great-Great grandson goes back in time to bombard the world with advertisements?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Works fine horizontally, but now the text leaves the box on vertical viewings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the heads up. Hm. Even horizontally?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate to be that guy, but formatting is broken on mobile. The tabs at the bottom start to clip in to the text making half of it illegible.

Looks like this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The history of advertisements is the story of their continual expansion into newly created spaces. Those spaces are becoming increasingly proximal and yet invasive by the same measure. This is a story about that trajectory mixed with the anomalous, and is a horror piece imo.

This is also in sort of thanks that even after 10 years, we don't have to suffer through ads on this site. Could be much worse folks.

All images are from Wiki Commons (see below).

Thanks to Cyantreuse for taking the initiative to improve the main image, great work, and to Leveritas & Countless Skies for their critique of the draft, which was prior to my decision to fashion this into a 4kon entry, so sorry if this is embarrassing.

Suggested viewing/listening: Faceshopping - SOPHIE (⚠️ Epilepsy warning ⚠️)

Suggested reading:
Society of the Spectacle - Guy Debord

Author’s Page
